metric_value: Two axes: Data Value (Y-axis: high to low) and Data Duration (X-axis: minutes to years)
notes: Defines the analytical framework for the positioning diagram

metric_value: High-End SCSI (Atlas); high-speed storage; direct/NAS/SAN attached; random data access; high I/O performance
notes: Short data duration (minutes to hours); high data value; premium tier

metric_value: MaXLine; medium-speed bulk storage; network attached; sequential & random data access; low cost per GB; moderate I/O performance
notes: Medium data duration (hours to weeks/months); declining data value; midline positioning

metric_value: Tape; low-speed storage; direct/SAN attached; sequential data access; removable media; low I/O performance
notes: Long data duration (months to years); low data value; archive tier

metric_value: Near-Line Storage / Content Storage; network attached; sequential and random access
notes: The slide explicitly creates the Nearline Storage and Content Storage labels as MaXLine's market categories

metric_value: High-end Performance Storage / On-Line Server; high-speed; direct/NAS/SAN attached; random access; high I/O
notes: Atlas clearly separated from MaXLine as the premium performance tier

metric_value: Off-Line Storage / Tape Library; low-speed; removable; sequential; low I/O
notes: Tape positioned as irreplaceable for deepest archive but clearly differentiated from disk-based tiers
Explicit cost advantage claim relative to Atlas/SCSI; does not specify ratio

metric_value: ATA positioned in spectrum below MaXLine toward consumer/low-reliability zone
notes: Inexpensive ATA shown but NOT labeled as a named tier — implies it is unsuitable for the enterprise data center

metric_value: A distinct disk-based nearline tier (MaXLine) will emerge between high-performance SCSI and tape in enterprise data centers
notes: Core market creation prediction: enterprises will adopt a third disk tier rather than rely on SCSI or tape alone
Deferred: verify whether dedicated nearline disk (SATA nearline) became a mainstream enterprise storage category by 2006-2010

metric_name: Data Value vs. Duration as universal ILM framework
metric_value: Two-axis (Data Value / Data Duration) model will be adopted as the standard way to classify storage tiers
notes: This framework matches cloud storage hot/warm/cold tier models used universally by 2015
Deferred: verify widespread adoption of value-duration axes in cloud and enterprise storage tier definitions

metric_value: Sequential and random data access (both) — unlike tape which is sequential-only
notes: Dual-access capability is MaXLine's key functional advantage over tape for recallable data

metric_value: Network attached (vs Atlas which is direct/NAS/SAN)
notes: NAS attachment implies NAS as the dominant MaXLine access protocol; aligns with file-based content storage use cases
